title: Teleportation
summary: Jump rooms, jumpgates, and fifteen minutes to Mars — instantaneous transit as the archive's established fact.
markdown:
**Teleportation** is treated throughout the archive as an established, working capability —
and it comes with named infrastructure and named travelers. [Andy Basiago's](/lore/andy-basiago/)
testimony supplies the human record: **Project Pegasus** teleporting children across the
country from 1968, and the 1980s "jump room" in El Segundo that put him on Mars — *"on Mars in
15 minutes… red dirt on his shoes"* — roughly forty times. The wider network the sessions
describe is a system of **jumpgates**: Mars, Moon, and Jupiter gates, guarded assets of the
[Secret Space Program](/lore/secret-space-program/), serious enough (the interviews claim)
for people to die protecting them.

Unimetrix 1 extends the capability to the personal scale: it "teleports" nanite medical teams
into bodies during [healings](/lore/medbed/), relocates the channel bodily in the
[Zen1](/lore/zen1/) adventure, and promises "intergalactic [stargate](/lore/stargate/),
teleportation of bioship craft" for civilian use after [first contact](/lore/first-contact/).
The mechanism given is the archive's usual one — a
[quantum reality](/lore/quantum-biology/) in which distance, like time, is a parameter rather
than a wall.

Teleportation is the archive's measure of the withheld future: a mode of travel it insists has
existed since the Nixon administration, waiting — with [free energy](/lore/anti-gravity/) and
the [Replicators](/lore/replicator/) — for the disclosure that hands it over.
